notes: Integrated from the real Grade 2 Quarter 3, Book 14 source file.
Strong Reader card: longer text and more inferential fable work.

book.title: The Oak and the Reed
book.description: The Oak and the Reed includes a six-chapter reading sequence, vocabulary, comprehension questions, journal/writing prompts, and assessment checks.

choiceGuidance.reason: longer text and more inferential fable work

weeks.title: The Oak and the Reed Book Card
reading: Chapter 6: The Lesson of the Storm
vocabulary: 1. encircle - to go completely around something
2. canopy - the upper layer of leaves and branches that covers like a roof
1. imposing - grand and impressive in appearance
2. unyielding - not giving way; stubborn and inflexible
1. condescension - an attitude of superiority toward someone you consider inferior
2. patronizing - treating someone as if they are less intelligent or important
1. resilient - able to recover quickly from difficulties
2. conviction - a strong belief or opinion
1. ominously - in a way that suggests something bad will happen
2. defiantly - in a bold way that shows resistance to authority or challenge
1. catastrophic - involving sudden great damage or suffering
2. fundamentally - in the most basic or essential way
journal: Write about whether it is better to be strong or flexible. Use the story to explain.
Compare the oak and the reed using character traits.
writing: Write about whether it is better to be strong or flexible. Use the story to explain.
Compare the oak and the reed using character traits.
questions: 1. How old was the oak tree?
2. What did other plants and trees do during storms?
1. How were the reeds different from the oak tree?
2. What did the reed do when strong winds blew?
1. How did the oak speak to the reed?
2. How did the reed respond to the oak's comments?
1. What did the reed say about his flexibility?
2. What did the reed suggest might be valuable?
1. What began to happen as the oak boasted about strength?
2. How did the oak respond to the approaching storm?
1. What happened to the oak tree during the storm?
2. What lesson did the storm teach?
assessment: Use chapter comprehension questions for daily checks.
Use journal/writing prompt as written-response evidence.
